recovery_sources: Operational Intelligence System (OIS) — Complete Feature and Function Deep Dive (2).docx
Operational Intelligence System (OIS) - Complete Feature and Function Deep Dive (2).docx

signals.name: Mid-shift 86 surprise
signals.description: Items go unavailable mid-service without early warning or substitution planning.
signals.source_ref: compass-market-analysis: intervention planning over fragmented tools

signals.name: Bar ticket delay
signals.description: Drink production holds back table progression or guest satisfaction.
signals.source_ref: deep-dive: service bottlenecks and throughput signals

signals.name: Closing reset failures
signals.description: The closing shift leaves unresolved prep, cleaning, or stock problems for the next day.
signals.source_ref: pre-seed-business-case: recurring operational drag between shifts

signals.name: Complaint response turns defensive
signals.description: Service failures trigger defensiveness, excuse-making, or inconsistent recovery instead of calm ownership and containment.
signals.source_ref: core-v2: recovery quality is visible in the first response to a guest-facing failure

signals.name: Inventory count variance
signals.description: Actual counts regularly differ from expected stock on hand.
signals.source_ref: compass-market-analysis: control loops and operator trust

signals.name: Critical role is left uncovered
signals.description: A role that protects flow or standards is missing entirely, forcing the rest of the venue to improvise around the gap.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: uncovered critical role expressed through restaurant service flow
evidence_types: no expo or floor lead in a pressured window
missing host, runner, or section owner

signals.name: Demand spike goes unabsorbed
signals.description: A sudden rush or wave of covers hits the venue faster than the operation can absorb without visible service strain.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: demand-capacity mismatch expressed through restaurant rush absorption
evidence_types: queue expands immediately after demand spike
service strain appears within minutes of surge

signals.name: Equipment breaks service flow
signals.description: Equipment or critical assets fail at the moment they are needed and force the service flow into delay, improvisation, or degraded quality.
signals.source_ref: core-v2: asset reliability should be treated as a live operational signal, not maintenance background

signals.name: Expo calls missing
signals.description: The pass lacks consistent verbal callouts for timing, fire, and pickup.
signals.source_ref: deep-dive: root-cause transparency between stations

signals.name: Follow-through is missing
signals.description: The venue discusses the right action, but the reset, check-back, or accountability loop does not actually land afterward.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: missing follow-through expressed through restaurant management rhythm

signals.name: Recurring guest complaints
signals.description: Complaint frequency indicates an unresolved service or product issue.
signals.source_ref: deep-dive: operational intake examples

signals.name: Handoff requires re-clarification
signals.description: A task or guest need has to be restated because the first handoff did not carry enough clarity to move forward cleanly.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: handoff failure expressed through restaurant pass and floor communication

signals.name: Improvement is not standardized
signals.description: The team finds a better way once, but it never becomes the normal way of operating across shifts or people.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: learning failure visible through failure to standardize wins

signals.name: Issue resolution is slow or unclear
signals.description: A guest issue or service exception is noticed, but the recovery path is hesitant, slow, or ambiguous.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: exception recovery weakness expressed through restaurant guest recovery

signals.name: Key operating metric is not reviewed
signals.description: The venue is not regularly looking at the metric that would reveal the current control problem before it becomes visible on the floor.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: control-loop weakness visible through missing metric review

signals.name: Last-minute volume shift hits the floor
signals.description: Reservations, walk-ins, large parties, or timing changes bunch together late enough to disrupt pacing and reset the guest flow.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: last-minute volume shift expressed through restaurant arrival flow
evidence_types: late group timing reshapes floor pacing

signals.name: Line check missing
signals.description: The line opens without a complete readiness check across product, equipment, and station setup.
signals.source_ref: deep-dive: diagnostic engine from opening risk to response

signals.name: Manager floor absence
signals.description: Managers are not visibly present during the venue's most critical service windows.
signals.source_ref: pre-seed-business-case: execution decays without leadership presence

signals.name: New staff depend on memory and verbal instruction
signals.description: Newer team members rely on remembering scattered verbal guidance because repeatable training structure is missing.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: capability gap visible through memory-based onboarding

signals.name: No live direction or reset occurs
signals.description: The shift visibly drifts under pressure and nobody steps in to reset pace, roles, or priorities in the moment.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: leadership rhythm failure expressed in live restaurant service

signals.name: No postmortem follows a breakdown
signals.description: The team survives a bad shift or incident, but there is no structured review to convert the pain into a system correction.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: learning failure visible through missing post-breakdown review
evidence_types: same breakdown treated as bad luck

signals.name: Opening checklist drift
signals.description: Opening standards vary enough that readiness depends on who opened the venue.
signals.source_ref: deep-dive: operator memory should not be the system

signals.name: Output quality is inconsistent
signals.description: The same menu item, service touchpoint, or guest interaction varies too much depending on who handled it or how busy the venue is.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: standards drift and capability variance in restaurant execution

signals.name: Ownership confusion in service
signals.description: Work stalls or doubles back because the team is unsure who owns the next action, escalation, or guest-facing decision.
signals.source_ref: core-v2: role clarity failures appear first as ambiguous ownership in live flow

signals.name: Peak period is undercovered
signals.description: The venue enters a known rush without enough capable coverage in the critical roles that carry flow.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: demand-capacity mismatch expressed through restaurant peak coverage

signals.name: Prep inconsistency
signals.description: Prep quality or completion varies by shift.
signals.source_ref: deep-dive: signals as surface symptoms

signals.name: Promised time is missed
signals.description: A quoted pickup, seating, delivery, or service time is missed badly enough to break guest trust.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: promise and expectation failure surfacing in restaurant operations

signals.name: Required inputs are missing at start
signals.description: Service starts without key prep, product, information, or setup inputs being ready when the team needs them.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: readiness failure visible through restaurant opening and line preparation

signals.name: Reservation pacing breakdown
signals.description: Reservation waves hit the floor without pacing rules or release logic.
signals.source_ref: deep-dive: intake examples and venue pressure points

signals.name: Rework or repeat work is observed
signals.description: The team is doing work twice, correcting preventable misses, or remaking steps that should have landed cleanly the first time.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: rework expressed through restaurant service and production execution
evidence_types: service steps repeated because first pass failed

signals.name: Same issue repeats without review
signals.description: The venue keeps reliving the same preventable problem because it is discussed informally but never closed through review and operating change.
signals.source_ref: core-v2: repeated unreviewed issues are direct evidence of a missing learning loop

signals.name: Same mistake repeats across people
signals.description: The same operational miss shows up across different team members, suggesting the issue lives in the system rather than in one person.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: repeated mistakes across people expressed through restaurant training and learning failure
evidence_types: error pattern travels across the team
different people repeat the same avoidable failure

signals.name: Section imbalance
signals.description: Some service sections overload while others remain underused.
signals.source_ref: pre-seed-business-case: labor inefficiency and execution drag

signals.name: Service delay
signals.description: Guests wait too long for the next service touchpoint.
signals.source_ref: deep-dive: operational intake and diagnostic engine

signals.name: Shift brief missing
signals.description: Managers are not running a structured pre-shift briefing.
signals.source_ref: deep-dive: welcome briefing and plans

signals.name: Shift or day starts unprepared
signals.description: The venue begins service without the minimum coordination, setup, and readiness needed to absorb normal pressure.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: shift starts unprepared expressed through restaurant readiness failures
evidence_types: team enters the shift chasing basics

signals.name: Skill mix is mismatched to the work
signals.description: The people present are not the right combination of capability and confidence for the pressure or complexity of the shift.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: capability mismatch expressed through restaurant shift deployment
evidence_types: experienced coverage too thin for the shift
complex station left to underprepared staff

signals.name: Staff confusion
signals.description: Team members show uncertainty about priorities or handoffs.
signals.source_ref: business-case: symptom blindness

signals.name: Standard steps skipped
signals.description: Critical service or prep steps are being skipped often enough that output consistency depends on who is working.
signals.source_ref: core-v2: standards drift is visible when basic steps stop being reliably repeated

signals.name: Table turn stall
signals.description: Tables remain uncleared or unseated long enough to slow service capacity.
signals.source_ref: pre-seed-business-case: symptom blindness in service flow

signals.name: Kitchen ticket bottleneck
signals.description: Tickets stack in production and delay flow through the kitchen.
signals.source_ref: consulting artifact: kitchen transformation

signals.name: Tool or system is unavailable when needed
signals.description: A device, system, or basic tool the venue relies on is missing, offline, dead, or otherwise unavailable during live work.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: tool or system unavailable expressed through restaurant live service friction
evidence_types: service tool missing at moment of use
system outage or device dead during shift

signals.name: Training is not translating into execution
signals.description: The team has been shown the standard, but the expected behavior still does not appear reliably in live service.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: training not translating to execution expressed through restaurant standards drift
evidence_types: trained behavior missing in live shift
coaching happened but standards still do not land

signals.name: Voids and comps rising
signals.description: The venue is losing margin through avoidable service recovery costs.
signals.source_ref: consulting artifact: volume decline and controls

signals.name: Waitlist estimate mismatch
signals.description: Quoted wait times do not match actual seating readiness.
signals.source_ref: deep-dive: operator trust depends on transparent signals

signals.name: Waste spike
signals.description: Unexpected food waste suggests prep, forecasting, or controls issues.
signals.source_ref: business-case: fragmented tool stack

signals.name: Work dies between roles
signals.description: A guest need or operating task stalls because everyone assumes someone else picked it up.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: role-boundary coordination failure in restaurant flow

signals.name: Work pace breaks under load
signals.description: The venue can move acceptably at low pressure, but once load rises the pace becomes visibly unstable and backlog forms quickly.
signals.source_ref: universal-signal-gold-set-01: work pace breaks under load expressed through restaurant floor execution

signals.name: Workaround has become normal
signals.description: The team routinely compensates for missing tools, broken equipment, or weak systems with improvised workarounds that are now treated as normal.
signals.source_ref: core-v2: normal workarounds are hidden evidence of tool friction and unclosed learning loops

failure_modes.name: Bar workflow breakdown
failure_modes.description: Bar setup, sequencing, and priority rules are not robust enough to support live service demand and floor handoffs.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: workflow and handoff failure expressed in beverage execution

failure_modes.name: Leadership briefing gap
failure_modes.description: Critical pre-service direction is missing, so the team starts without a shared read on pressure points, priorities, or constraints.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: leadership rhythm failure expressed through pre-shift briefing absence

failure_modes.name: Control loop gap
failure_modes.description: Void, comp, waste, stock, and other operational leakage are visible but not reviewed tightly enough to drive correction.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: control-loop failure expressed through restaurant operating controls

failure_modes.name: Deployment misalignment
failure_modes.description: Coverage, sectioning, and peak-role placement do not match the real demand pattern or skill mix of the shift.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: demand-capacity mismatch expressed in FOH deployment

failure_modes.name: Equipment reliability gap
failure_modes.description: Critical equipment or operational assets cannot be relied on during the moments that matter most to service flow.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: asset reliability failure expressed through live service disruption

failure_modes.name: Guest recovery breakdown
failure_modes.description: The venue does not recover guest-facing failures with enough ownership, clarity, consistency, or follow-up to restore trust cleanly.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: exception recovery failure expressed through poor complaint handling

failure_modes.name: Host flow breakdown
failure_modes.description: Quoting, pacing, seating release, and reservation handling are not tight enough to shape arrival demand cleanly.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: expectation-setting and handoff logic in hospitality intake

failure_modes.name: Inventory accuracy gap
failure_modes.description: Counts, depletion updates, and 86 status are not trustworthy enough for service teams to act with confidence.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: commercial discipline and control-loop weakness in stock truth

failure_modes.name: Learning loop absence
failure_modes.description: The venue experiences the same recurring breakdowns without a short review loop that turns incidents into changed behavior, standards, or checks.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: learning failure expressed through repeated unresolved operational pain

failure_modes.name: Manager presence gap
failure_modes.description: Managers are not visibly positioned where live reset, coaching, and escalation are needed during high-risk moments.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: leadership rhythm failure expressed through floor presence

failure_modes.name: Menu complexity overload
failure_modes.description: The menu or service promise asks the operation to carry more complexity than the current system can execute reliably.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: expectation and complexity failure expressed through the offer

failure_modes.name: Open-close control gap
failure_modes.description: Opening and closing are treated as chores instead of enforced control loops that protect the next service cycle.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: readiness and control-loop failures expressed through reset discipline

failure_modes.name: Pass communication gap
failure_modes.description: Timing language, pickup ownership, and cross-station handoff rules at the pass are too weak to move food cleanly.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: handoff failure expressed in kitchen-to-floor transfer

failure_modes.name: Prep system breakdown
failure_modes.description: Prep logic, depletion visibility, and replenishment discipline are not strong enough to keep service execution stable.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: readiness failure expressed through prep and replenishment

failure_modes.name: Role ownership blur
failure_modes.description: Critical actions, escalations, or service decisions do not have ownership that is explicit enough to hold under live pressure.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: role clarity failure expressed through live service ownership ambiguity

failure_modes.name: Service standard drift
failure_modes.description: The venue has nominal standards, but daily execution has drifted far enough that output consistency depends on who is on shift.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: standards drift expressed through hospitality variation and skipped basics

failure_modes.name: Shift readiness gap
failure_modes.description: The venue enters service without a reliable readiness check across stations, people, stock, and live priorities.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: readiness failure expressed through service start conditions

failure_modes.name: Tooling friction
failure_modes.description: Work is repeatedly slowed, improvised, or made more fragile because the tools, systems, or information surfaces do not support the real operating flow cleanly.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: tool or resource friction expressed through repeated workarounds

failure_modes.name: Insufficient training protocol
failure_modes.description: Standards, role expectations, and recovery behaviors are not reinforced enough to survive into live service execution.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: capability gap expressed through hospitality team training

failure_modes.name: Undefined workflow structure
failure_modes.description: The service sequence, decision rules, and escalation logic are too implicit to hold under live pressure.
failure_modes.source_ref: core-v2: workflow design failure expressed through restaurant service flow

response_patterns.name: Bar execution reset
response_patterns.description: Re-sequence bar setup, priority rules, and service handoff so beverage flow holds under peak pressure.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: redesign workflow at the beverage node

response_patterns.name: Deployment reset
response_patterns.description: Rebuild sectioning, role placement, and peak-window coverage around the real shape of service demand.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: align roles and coverage in the live venue

response_patterns.name: Host pacing reset
response_patterns.description: Constrain quoting, arrival pacing, and seating release so front-door promises stay inside actual capacity.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: constrain promises and strengthen demand shaping at the door

response_patterns.name: Inventory accuracy reset
response_patterns.description: Make counts, 86 logic, and depletion updates trustworthy enough that service decisions stop relying on guesswork.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: tighten control loops and restore commercial discipline

response_patterns.name: Leadership rhythm reset
response_patterns.description: Create repeatable pre-shift, in-shift, and post-shift leadership cadence so the floor gets real direction and closure.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: install operating rhythm for hospitality leadership

response_patterns.name: Learning loop reset
response_patterns.description: Install a short review loop that turns repeated issues into explicit next actions, standards, or system fixes.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: verify and learn from repeated operational breakdowns

response_patterns.name: Manager presence reset
response_patterns.description: Put visible management attention where service risk is actually building, not where it is most comfortable to stand.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: align leadership coverage with real operating pressure

response_patterns.name: Offer complexity trim
response_patterns.description: Reduce menu or service complexity that the current operation cannot execute without repeated instability.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: reduce operational complexity when the offer is outrunning execution capacity

response_patterns.name: Open-close reset
response_patterns.description: Turn opening and closing into non-negotiable readiness and control rituals that protect the next cycle of work.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: restore readiness through enforced operating rhythm

response_patterns.name: Pass communication reset
response_patterns.description: Install explicit timing language, handoff rules, and pickup discipline across the pass.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: strengthen handoffs at the kitchen-floor boundary

response_patterns.name: Prep and control reset
response_patterns.description: Restore prep visibility, depletion awareness, and live control points before service instability compounds.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: restore readiness and tighten control loops around prep

response_patterns.name: Recovery protocol reset
response_patterns.description: Install a consistent recovery sequence so guest-facing failures are contained with ownership, calmness, and follow-up.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: strengthen recovery through a repeatable protocol

response_patterns.name: Reliability restore
response_patterns.description: Expose recurring breakers, remove normalized workarounds, and restore the tools or assets the flow depends on.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: remove friction and restore reliability at the moment of operational need

response_patterns.name: Role visibility reset
response_patterns.description: Make ownership, escalation, and live responsibility visible enough that ambiguous work stops dying between people.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: stabilize visibility around who owns what next

response_patterns.name: Service flow reset
response_patterns.description: Make the core service sequence explicit, visible, and executable under real pressure.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: redesign workflow expressed through service flow

response_patterns.name: Standard work reset
response_patterns.description: Reinforce a small number of critical operating standards until execution variation narrows and consistency returns.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: standardize execution through observed reinforcement, not generic reminders

response_patterns.name: Training reset
response_patterns.description: Move standards, role clarity, and recovery behavior out of memory and into reinforced execution practice.
response_patterns.source_ref: core-v2: build capability and standardize execution

blocks.name: Create bar priority lane
blocks.description: Separate the drink tickets that are actively blocking table flow from the rest of the bar queue so urgent beverage work becomes visible and protected.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: redesign workflow at the beverage node by exposing table-critical priority work
entry_conditions: Bar tickets are delaying table progression or guests are waiting on drinks that gate the next service step.
The venue can distinguish truly table-critical tickets from normal queue volume.
contraindications: Do not create a second queue without explicit rules for what qualifies as priority work.
Do not use this lane to hide structural understaffing or broken bar setup.
proof_of_completion: A visible priority lane exists for bar work that blocks table progression.
The bar team can explain the rule for entering and clearing priority tickets.
At least one pressured service shows faster resolution of table-critical drink tickets.

blocks.name: Build arrival pacing board
blocks.description: Create a live board for reservation waves, walk-in pressure, seat release timing, and decision points so the front door can shape demand in real time.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: stabilize visibility and constrain promises at the intake surface
entry_conditions: Hosts are making pacing decisions without a shared live view of incoming pressure and seating release.
Wait quote logic exists or is being installed alongside the board.
contraindications: Do not build a board no one updates during service.
Do not make the board so detailed that hosts abandon it in real time.
proof_of_completion: The host team has a single live board for reservation waves, walk-ins, and release decisions.
The board is updated through at least one real pressure window.
Hosts use the board to justify quote and pacing changes instead of relying on guesswork.

blocks.name: Build floor training checklist
blocks.description: Convert critical service behaviors, role expectations, and recovery standards into an explicit onboarding and refresh checklist that managers can coach against.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: build capability by making live standards coachable instead of memory-dependent
entry_conditions: The same service mistakes repeat across people or new starters rely on shadowing alone.
Role expectations and standard behaviors are already explicit enough to train against.
contraindications: Do not turn training into a document dump with no live signoff or coaching.
Do not build the checklist before role ownership and service standards are clear.
proof_of_completion: A role-specific training checklist exists for the floor positions that most affect service quality.
Each critical behavior has an observation or signoff method, not just a checkbox.
Managers use the checklist during onboarding or refresh rather than informal memory transfer.

blocks.name: Capture breakers and workarounds
blocks.description: Create a simple reliability board for recurring equipment failures, missing tools, and normalized workarounds so the venue can assign fixes before the next shift absorbs them again.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: reliability improves when hidden workarounds and repeated breakers become visible and owned
entry_conditions: The venue is repeatedly improvising around missing tools, broken equipment, or unreliable systems.
Someone can own each breaker and decide whether it is fixed, replaced, or escalated.
contraindications: Do not log breakers without assigning a real fix owner and deadline.
Do not normalize workarounds by documenting them unless the underlying issue is also escalated.
proof_of_completion: A visible breaker and workaround log exists with owner, status, and next action.
Recurring tool or equipment issues are reviewed before the next service cycle.
At least one normalized workaround has been removed or escalated through the log.

blocks.name: Run closing reset check
blocks.description: Install a non-negotiable closing reset across prep, stock, cleanliness, and unresolved issues so the next service cycle does not begin with inherited fragility.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: restore readiness by turning the close into a true reset loop
entry_conditions: The next shift inherits missing prep, poor cleanliness, stock gaps, or unresolved actions.
A close owner can verify reset completion before people leave.
contraindications: Do not rely on self-attestation without a visible check and ownership loop.
Do not close unresolved blockers without explicitly passing them into the next operating cycle.
proof_of_completion: A closing reset checklist exists with named ownership and verification.
Unresolved issues are either fixed before close or passed forward explicitly.
The next open shows reduced inherited friction from the previous shift.

blocks.name: Create station role matrix
blocks.description: Turn the mapped service flow into explicit primary ownership, backup ownership, and escalation paths at each station so work stops dying between roles.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: align roles and coverage after workflow reality is made explicit
entry_conditions: The venue has a current service map or another reliable view of the real operating flow.
Role ambiguity, double-handling, or stalled ownership is visible during service.
contraindications: Do not assign roles before the actual flow and handoffs are understood.
Do not overcomplicate the matrix with edge cases that the team will not use live.
proof_of_completion: Every key station has named primary and backup ownership.
Escalation routes for blocked service moments are visible to the team.
The matrix has been briefed to the floor and validated in one live shift.

blocks.name: Launch daily 86 board
blocks.description: Create a visible pre-service 86 and depletion board so stock risk, substitutions, and item status changes are shared before they become guest-facing surprises.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: stabilize visibility around stock truth before live service multiplies confusion
entry_conditions: 86 surprises or uncertain substitutions are reaching the floor mid-service.
The venue has at least a limited count or depletion truth to feed the board.
contraindications: Do not post unreliable stock information that will be disproved immediately.
Do not keep the board hidden from the roles that need to act on it.
proof_of_completion: A visible 86 board exists before service with status, substitutions, and risk items.
Frontline roles know where to read it and when it updates.
Mid-shift item surprises decline because decisions are shared earlier.

blocks.name: Launch daily waste review
blocks.description: Review daily waste incidents, their causes, and the next corrective action so prep loss stops being treated as normal background noise.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: tighten control loops by forcing review and correction of recurring waste
entry_conditions: Waste is spiking or prep overproduction is visible but not reviewed systematically.
The team can identify what was wasted, why, and who owns the follow-up.
contraindications: Do not run a waste review that records loss without changing prep or ordering behavior.
Do not bury avoidable waste inside broad end-of-day admin.
proof_of_completion: Waste incidents are logged daily with causes and follow-up actions.
The review distinguishes controllable waste from true demand noise.
Prep or ordering changes are being made based on the reviewed loss patterns.

blocks.name: Define wait quote rules
blocks.description: Define the decision rules for quoting waits, protecting buffer, tightening promises, and escalating when seating readiness slips.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: constrain promises so the front door stops selling false timing
entry_conditions: Guests are surprised by quoted waits or seating timing.
Hosts currently rely on feel rather than an explicit quoting logic.
contraindications: Do not define rules using ideal table-turn assumptions that the venue does not actually achieve.
Do not create a rule set that only the strongest host can interpret.
proof_of_completion: Quote ranges, buffers, release rules, and escalation triggers are written in plain language.
Hosts can explain when to widen, hold, or reset promised waits.
The quote guide has been used live in at least one pressured service period.

blocks.name: Create guest recovery loop
blocks.description: Install a clear recovery script, ownership rule, and follow-up loop so service failures are absorbed cleanly and turned into corrected operating behavior.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: strengthen recovery and verify learning from repeated guest-facing failures
entry_conditions: Complaints, defensive responses, or inconsistent goodwill decisions are recurring.
Leaders can review recovery quality and close the loop on repeated causes.
contraindications: Do not launch the recovery loop if managers will not review how recoveries actually played out.
Do not confuse apology language with true containment, ownership, and recurrence prevention.
proof_of_completion: A common recovery sequence exists for acknowledge, own, resolve, and follow up.
Frontline teams know when to recover directly and when to escalate.
Recurring complaint themes are logged and reviewed for underlying fixes.

blocks.name: Run host reservation huddle
blocks.description: Run a short pre-peak huddle for the host position to align pacing, quoted waits, reservation releases, and escalation when arrival pressure changes shape.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: constrain promises through local operating rhythm at the front door
entry_conditions: Quoted waits or reservation releases regularly drift away from real seating readiness.
The venue has identifiable arrival waves or pressure windows that can be planned for.
contraindications: Do not run the huddle without current pacing information or authority to change the release plan.
Do not use a host huddle to mask broken floor-turn or bussing fundamentals.
proof_of_completion: Peak arrival windows have a named huddle moment and owner.
The host team reviews quote logic, reservation pacing, pressure points, and escalation rules before the wave hits.
The venue can show how quote or release decisions changed during at least one real peak.

blocks.name: Install expo callouts
blocks.description: Install a minimum shared pass language for fire, hold, pickup, and delay escalation so kitchen-to-floor handoffs stop relying on guesswork and volume.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: strengthen handoffs with explicit transfer language at the service boundary
entry_conditions: Food is dying at the pass, pickup timing is unclear, or calls are inconsistent.
There is enough line leadership to enforce the callout standard for several services.
contraindications: Do not add more language than the pass can hold live under pressure.
Do not treat this as training theater if no one will coach or reinforce it in service.
proof_of_completion: A minimum callout vocabulary for fire, hold, pickup, and delays is posted at the pass.
Expo and floor leaders have rehearsed the timing language in a live service window.
Pickup misses and re-clarification moments are being tracked after rollout.

blocks.name: Install pre-shift briefing
blocks.description: Create a repeatable pre-service briefing that aligns the team on demand shape, risks, 86s, role pressure points, and escalation rules before the floor gets noisy.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: install operating rhythm before asking the team for more precision
entry_conditions: Service starts with uneven direction, repeated surprises, or unclear priorities.
A leader is available to run the briefing and hold follow-through.
contraindications: Do not turn the briefing into a long speech with no operational decisions.
Do not use this as a substitute for fixing missing role clarity or readiness basics.
proof_of_completion: A fixed briefing cadence, owner, and timing now exist.
The team receives covers, risks, 86s, pressure points, and focus items before service.
Escalation ownership for the shift is named out loud and visible.

blocks.name: Launch inventory count cycle
blocks.description: Install a count cadence for high-risk lines so stock truth, 86 decisions, and loss reviews are built on trusted numbers instead of intuition.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: tighten control loops around the items most likely to distort service or margin
entry_conditions: Counts drift, 86 surprises, or stock variance already affect service confidence.
The venue can identify a limited high-risk count set instead of counting everything poorly.
contraindications: Do not launch a count cycle with no variance review or ownership loop behind it.
Do not start with full-inventory perfection when only a few lines drive most of the damage.
proof_of_completion: A fixed cadence, owner, and scope exist for high-risk inventory lines.
Variance thresholds and follow-up actions are documented.
Count output now feeds 86 decisions or control reviews rather than sitting unused.

blocks.name: Run manager floor walk
blocks.description: Run a timed manager walk during critical windows to catch developing pressure, coach live execution, and trigger escalation before the floor drifts into unmanaged chaos.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: leadership rhythm should place attention where risk is building, not where it is comfortable
entry_conditions: Managers disappear into admin or static positions while service risk builds on the floor.
The venue has clear peak windows where visible management attention changes outcomes.
contraindications: Do not turn the floor walk into passive observation with no intervention logic.
Do not schedule the walk if the manager has no authority to coach or escalate live issues.
proof_of_completion: Managers have a timed floor walk cadence for critical windows.
The walk includes observation targets, coaching prompts, and escalation checks.
At least one real service shows the manager redirecting attention based on the walk.

blocks.name: Set manager peak-window plan
blocks.description: Define where the manager stands, what they watch, and what triggers intervention during the venue's highest-risk service windows.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: leadership coverage should be designed around pressure, not habit
entry_conditions: Peak periods repeatedly collapse because leadership attention is not placed where the risk actually concentrates.
The venue can identify its critical windows, pressure nodes, and likely intervention points.
contraindications: Do not create a peak-window plan if the manager is routinely pulled into unrelated admin at the same time.
Do not use vague instructions like 'watch the floor' without explicit triggers and zones.
proof_of_completion: A written peak-window plan exists with positions, watch zones, and intervention triggers.
Manager coverage is aligned to the real pressure pattern rather than static presence habits.
The plan is used in at least one live peak and adjusted based on what happened.

blocks.name: Map the service steps
blocks.description: Capture the real guest-facing service sequence, handoff boundaries, and failure-prone moments so workflow redesign starts from observed reality instead of opinion.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: redesign workflow by observing the real service loop before assigning fixes
entry_conditions: Recurring service delay or complaint patterns suggest the real flow is poorly understood.
Leaders can observe at least one representative service window before redesigning roles or standards.
contraindications: Do not start with workshop theory alone when no live observation has been done.
Do not treat this as a paperwork exercise during an active peak collapse.
proof_of_completion: A timestamped service map exists from arrival to payment and reset.
Each handoff point has a named owner and at least one observed friction note.
Three highest-risk flow breaks are identified for intervention sequencing.

blocks.name: Trim offer complexity
blocks.description: Identify the menu or service elements creating disproportionate operational instability and simplify them until the current system can execute reliably again.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: complexity should be reduced when the promise outruns the operation's current strength
entry_conditions: A subset of menu or service elements creates repeated instability, remakes, or bottlenecks.
Leadership is willing to remove, pause, simplify, or resequence parts of the offer.
contraindications: Do not trim complexity without linking decisions to observed execution pain and value impact.
Do not keep everything sacred when the operation is clearly unable to carry it.
proof_of_completion: A complexity review exists showing which offer elements create disproportionate drag.
At least one menu or service element has been simplified, paused, or resequenced.
The venue can explain why the trimmed complexity should improve execution reliability.

blocks.name: Run opening line readiness check
blocks.description: Install a structured line-opening check across product, equipment, station setup, 86 risk, and immediate blockers so service does not begin on hidden fragility.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: restore readiness before live execution compounds preventable instability
entry_conditions: The line starts service with missing prep, unclear 86s, or surprise equipment issues.
Someone with authority can stop the open long enough to resolve red conditions.
contraindications: Do not convert this into a box-ticking ritual without stop/go rules.
Do not open the line while critical blockers remain unresolved but undocumented.
proof_of_completion: A visible readiness check exists for station setup, product, equipment, and 86 risk.
Red conditions have explicit escalation and open-delay logic.
The line has completed the check before at least one live service start.

blocks.name: Run post-shift learning review
blocks.description: Run a short post-shift review of repeated breakdowns, what actually happened, and what changes before the next cycle so problems stop coming back unchallenged.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: learning compounds only when incidents are turned into changed operating truth quickly
entry_conditions: The same preventable issue is recurring and the venue can review it while details are still fresh.
A leader is willing to close the loop with explicit next actions rather than a vague debrief.
contraindications: Do not turn the review into emotional venting with no operating change.
Do not review every issue every night; focus on repeated or high-cost breakdowns.
proof_of_completion: A short post-shift review record exists with issue, cause, and next change.
At least one repeated issue has a named owner and explicit follow-up before the next shift.
The review distinguishes noise from recurring systemic problems.

blocks.name: Rebalance sections
blocks.description: Redraw section boundaries, support roles, and workload assumptions around the actual cover flow so the peak period stops collapsing into uneven pressure.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: align roles and coverage to the true demand pattern instead of legacy sectioning
entry_conditions: Sections overload unevenly while other capacity sits underused.
The team can identify the venue's real peak windows, service mix, and pressure points.
contraindications: Do not redraw sections without evidence from actual cover flow and support demands.
Do not use static fairness logic when the venue's traffic pattern is structurally uneven.
proof_of_completion: Section map reflects actual guest flow and support load, not habit.
Primary and support assignments are visible before the peak window starts.
Rebalance triggers are defined for overload moments.

blocks.name: Install standard work observation loop
blocks.description: Choose a small number of critical service standards, observe them live, coach misses in the moment, and review drift at the end of the shift.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: standards only tighten when they are observed and coached in live service
entry_conditions: A few critical standards are worth protecting because drift is visible and costly.
A leader can observe and coach during live service without disappearing into admin.
contraindications: Do not observe ten standards at once when the team can only absorb two or three.
Do not treat the loop as punitive scorekeeping instead of live correction.
proof_of_completion: A short standard observation sheet exists for live service use.
At least one shift has completed observe, coach, and review against the chosen standards.
The venue can point to repeated drift patterns and the coaching responses used.

blocks.name: Standardize prep sheet
blocks.description: Create a role-based prep sheet with clear quantities, cutoffs, and ownership so prep stops depending on memory and shift-to-shift interpretation.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: standardize execution and readiness through explicit prep ownership
entry_conditions: Prep quality or completion varies materially by shift or person.
The venue can identify critical prep lines and the roles that own them.
contraindications: Do not create a prep sheet with unrealistic quantities or no cutoff logic.
Do not treat the sheet as complete unless prep ownership is visible and reviewable.
proof_of_completion: A role-based prep sheet exists with named owners, quantities, and cutoffs.
Prep completion can be checked before service instead of assumed.
Shift-to-shift variation in prep readiness is reduced through shared standards.

blocks.name: Run void and comp review huddle
blocks.description: Review avoidable voids and comps in a short huddle so the venue closes the loop on loss incidents instead of absorbing them as normal service cost.
blocks.source_ref: core-v2: tighten control loops by making avoidable loss visible, reviewed, and owned
entry_conditions: Voids or comps are rising and the venue cannot explain which are avoidable.
A leader can review incidents with enough detail to assign corrective actions.
contraindications: Do not use the huddle to blame staff without identifying process causes.
Do not review losses if the venue will not track whether follow-up actions were completed.
proof_of_completion: A short review cadence exists for avoidable voids and comps.
Each reviewed incident has a likely cause and named follow-up action.
The venue can distinguish repeatable process losses from true one-off exceptions.

tools.name: 86 board template
tools.description: Visible status board for depletion risk, substitutions, and item availability decisions before they reach the guest as surprise.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: stock truth should be shared before service pressure turns it into confusion
tools.usage_moment: Use before service and update whenever item availability materially changes.
tools.expected_output: A visible list of 86 items, depletion risks, substitutions, and sell-through priorities.

tools.name: Arrival pacing board
tools.description: Live board for reservation waves, walk-ins, seat release timing, and front-door pacing decisions.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: intake shaping needs a shared live surface, not private judgment
tools.usage_moment: Use during service to track arrival pressure and seat release decisions in real time.
tools.expected_output: A current front-door view of waves, waits, release logic, and emerging pacing risk.

tools.name: Bar priority board
tools.description: Visual lane that isolates the drinks most likely to block guest flow, table turns, or recovery moments if they sit in the general queue.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: priority surfaces should make blocking work unmistakably visible
tools.usage_moment: Use during live bar service when certain tickets need protected handling to preserve table flow.
tools.expected_output: A short visible queue of table-critical drink tickets with explicit entry and clearing rules.

tools.name: Breaker and workaround log
tools.description: Visible log of repeated equipment failures, missing tools, system frictions, and the workarounds the team is using to survive them.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: reliability restoration starts by exposing the breakers the team has normalized
tools.usage_moment: Use whenever recurring breakers or workarounds surface during readiness checks or live service.
tools.expected_output: A visible reliability list with issue, workaround, owner, urgency, and next corrective action.

tools.name: Closing reset checklist
tools.description: Close-down checklist covering stock, prep, cleanliness, unresolved issues, and anything that would poison the next day's readiness if left implicit.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: reset discipline should preserve the next operating cycle, not just finish the current one
tools.usage_moment: Use at close before team members leave and before unresolved issues are handed over.
tools.expected_output: A signed close record with completed reset items, unresolved blockers, and explicit carry-forward actions.

tools.name: Complexity trim matrix
tools.description: Decision matrix for identifying which menu or service elements create disproportionate execution drag relative to value and should be simplified first.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: complexity should be trimmed with explicit tradeoff logic, not instinct alone
tools.usage_moment: Use when the current offer is creating repeated instability and leadership is reviewing simplification options.
tools.expected_output: A ranked view of high-complexity, low-defensibility offer elements with proposed simplification actions.

tools.name: Expo callout card
tools.description: Compact pass reference showing the minimum shared language for fire, hold, pickup, delays, and escalation at the kitchen-to-floor boundary.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: handoff language should be explicit enough to survive noise and speed
tools.usage_moment: Use at the pass during live service until the callout standard is internalized.
tools.expected_output: A shared pass script that reduces re-clarification and missed pickup signals.

tools.name: Floor walk sheet
tools.description: Timed observation sheet for live coaching, escalation checks, guest-flow pressure, and the moments where a manager should intervene rather than watch passively.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: management presence should be guided by real risk signals, not vague attentiveness
tools.usage_moment: Use during critical service windows while the manager is moving the floor deliberately.
tools.expected_output: A short record of observed pressure points, coaching actions, escalation events, and follow-up needs.

tools.name: Guest recovery script
tools.description: Recovery script covering acknowledgement, ownership, immediate resolution, goodwill boundary, and follow-up so service failures are contained consistently.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: recovery should combine warmth, ownership, and recurrence prevention
tools.usage_moment: Use immediately after a service failure or complaint that requires active recovery and documented follow-up.
tools.expected_output: A completed recovery action with named owner, resolution path, and follow-up or learning note.

tools.name: Host huddle template
tools.description: Short huddle template for reviewing reservation waves, quote logic, arrival pressure, and escalation rules before the front door gets stressed.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: local operating rhythm at intake should shape promises, not just react to them
tools.usage_moment: Use 10 to 15 minutes before peak arrivals or any major reservation wave.
tools.expected_output: A brief pacing huddle with quote decisions, pressure notes, and a named escalation owner.

tools.name: Inventory count sheet
tools.description: High-risk count sheet that focuses on the lines most likely to cause service disruption, margin leakage, or false 86 decisions.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: tighter control loops begin with a narrow, trusted counting surface
tools.usage_moment: Use on the defined cadence for high-risk lines before count truth feeds service and control decisions.
tools.expected_output: A variance-reviewed count record with follow-up actions and any resulting 86 or replenishment decisions.

tools.name: Line readiness check
tools.description: Stop-go checklist for product availability, equipment readiness, station setup, 86 risk, and any blocker that should delay the open.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: readiness tools should create real stop/go decisions, not ceremonial ticks
tools.usage_moment: Use before opening the line and after any disruption that could invalidate readiness.
tools.expected_output: A signed readiness record with open blockers, resolved blockers, and clear escalation for red conditions.

tools.name: Peak-window plan
tools.description: Simple plan that fixes where the manager stands, what they watch, and what triggers intervention during the venue's highest-risk windows.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: leadership deployment needs a visible plan tied to real pressure moments
tools.usage_moment: Use before and during the venue's highest-risk service windows.
tools.expected_output: A written manager coverage plan with zones, watch targets, and intervention triggers.

tools.name: Post-shift learning review
tools.description: Short review template for repeated issues, what actually happened, what changes next, and who owns the follow-up before the next service cycle begins.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: learning loops need a short, repeatable review surface tied to action
tools.usage_moment: Use after service when repeated or high-cost breakdowns deserve same-day learning closure.
tools.expected_output: A concise review with issue, cause hypothesis, next change, and named owner.

tools.name: Prep sheet template
tools.description: Role-based prep tracker with quantities, cutoffs, owner names, and completion visibility for the items that protect service readiness.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: readiness improves when prep becomes explicit, visible, and reviewable
tools.usage_moment: Use before service and during prep handovers to verify readiness against explicit ownership.
tools.expected_output: A prep tracker showing what is done, what is not, who owns it, and what cutoff or quantity applies.

tools.name: Section deployment board
tools.description: Visible sectioning board that shows primary server assignments, support coverage, overload triggers, and rebalance options.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: deployment reset needs a visible operating shape the team can act on immediately
tools.usage_moment: Use before the peak and update when live load materially changes section pressure.
tools.expected_output: A live section board with named ownership, support roles, and rebalance triggers.

tools.name: Service observation sheet
tools.description: Timestamped worksheet for capturing service touchpoints, delay points, unclear handoffs, and visible recovery failures during live observation.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: evidence-first workflow redesign starts with observation, not memory
tools.usage_moment: Use during a representative live service before redesigning workflow or role ownership.
tools.expected_output: A marked-up service map with timestamps, handoff friction notes, and three highest-risk failure moments.

tools.name: Shift brief template
tools.description: Structured briefing template covering demand shape, 86s, live risks, role pressure points, recovery focus, and named escalation ownership.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: leadership rhythm should create shared situational awareness before the shift gets loud
tools.usage_moment: Use at the start of each shift and before major peak windows.
tools.expected_output: A short briefing record with priorities, risks, ownership, and the shift's operational focus.

tools.name: Standard work observation sheet
tools.description: Short observation sheet for tracking whether critical service standards are actually being executed, coached, and reinforced in live service.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: standardization depends on visible observation, not memory of training
tools.usage_moment: Use during live service when checking a small set of critical standards and coaching misses.
tools.expected_output: A short record of observed standards, misses, coaching actions, and repeated drift patterns.

tools.name: Station role matrix
tools.description: Role matrix that shows primary ownership, backup ownership, escalation paths, and cross-station dependencies for the live floor.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: role clarity needs visible ownership, not verbal assumptions
tools.usage_moment: Use after service flow mapping and before briefing the team on new role boundaries.
tools.expected_output: A visible station matrix with named owners, backups, and escalation routes for blocked moments.

tools.name: Training checklist
tools.description: Role-specific training checklist with live signoff points so new and existing team members can be coached against explicit service standards.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: capability grows when standards become observable and signoff-able
tools.usage_moment: Use during onboarding, refresh training, and manager coaching for service roles.
tools.expected_output: A signed checklist of critical behaviors, observations, and capability gaps by role.

tools.name: Void review template
tools.description: Structured review template for voids and comps that captures the incident, likely cause, preventability, and the corrective action to assign.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: control loops only tighten when loss incidents are explained and owned
tools.usage_moment: Use during the void and comp review huddle after avoidable loss incidents are identified.
tools.expected_output: A short review record with cause, preventability judgment, and named corrective action.

tools.name: Wait quote guide
tools.description: Decision guide for quoted waits, timing buffers, release logic, and escalation when seating readiness and promises drift apart.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: expectation-setting needs explicit rules rather than personality-driven guesses
tools.usage_moment: Use whenever waits are quoted, held, widened, or reset during live service.
tools.expected_output: A plain-language rule set for quote ranges, buffer use, and escalation thresholds.

tools.name: Waste log
tools.description: Daily log for waste incidents, likely causes, and the next correction so recurring prep loss becomes visible enough to fix.
tools.source_ref: core-v2: loss visibility should feed learning and action, not passive record-keeping
tools.usage_moment: Use at end of day or after meaningful waste events to support a short loss review.
tools.expected_output: A dated waste record with cause notes, pattern tags, and the next corrective action.
